The family has grown to include stencil and slab variants, and was recognised with a D&AD Yellow Pencil in 2014 and the Tokyo Type Directors Club Typeface Design prize in 2015.

name: Independent Sans Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed cut of a newspaper sans-serif, sharing proportions with the broader Independent family; the narrow width and functional weight range place it firmly in the grotesque tradition suited to editorial column settings.
summary: The condensed cut of A2-Type's commissioned type system for The Independent newspaper, designed in 2013 for tight print and digital column work.
description: Part of a large interconnected type family built for The Independent's daily print and digital editions, this condensed sans shares underlying structure and proportions with the wider Independent suite. Five weights run from Light to Black, suited to headline stacking and space-constrained layouts. The broader Independent family won a D&AD Yellow Pencil in 2014 and the Typeface Design prize at the Tokyo TDC in 2015.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe the Independent family as a comprehensive suite of typefaces designed in 2013 for The Independent newspaper's print and digital editions, conceived as a set of interconnected typefaces sharing the same proportions and underlying structure. The condensed cuts sit alongside three master sets of Serif, Sans and Condensed, with additional Stencil and Slab variants added later to broaden the family's range of applications.

name: Independent Sans Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil variant of a humanist sans designed for newspaper use; the stencil cuts create interrupted strokes while retaining the proportions of the underlying sans family.
summary: A stencil cut of the Independent newspaper's sans-serif family, sharing the same proportions as the broader Independent type system designed in 2013.
description: Part of a large type system originally commissioned for The Independent newspaper's print and digital editions, this stencil variant extends the family into more expressive display territory. Five weights run from Light to Black, each carrying the interrupted stroke pattern typical of stencil design while remaining structurally consistent with the companion sans. The broader Independent family received a D&AD Yellow Pencil in 2014 and the Typeface Design prize at the Tokyo TDC in 2015.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe the Independent family as a comprehensive suite of typefaces designed in 2013 for The Independent newspaper's daily print and digital editions. The fonts were conceived as a set of interconnected typefaces sharing the same proportions and underlying structure. The Sans Stencil was later added to broaden the family's range of applications.

name: Independent Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif designed as part of a larger newspaper type system, sharing proportions with the serif and sans counterparts; the uniform bracketed slabs and sturdy weight range place it firmly in the slab serif tradition suited to print and editorial use.
summary: A slab serif drawn as part of A2-Type's commissioned type system for The Independent newspaper, covering five weights with matching italics for print and digital use.
description: Designed in 2013 as one member of a larger interconnected family built for The Independent's daily print and digital editions, the Slab shares underlying proportions with the accompanying serif and sans cuts. Five weights run from Light to Black, each with a companion italic, giving editors a full range for body text through to section headings. The broader Independent family earned a D&AD Yellow Pencil in 2014 and the Typeface Design prize at the Tokyo TDC in 2015.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Independent Slab as part of a comprehensive suite of typefaces designed in 2013 for The Independent newspaper's daily print and digital editions. The fonts were conceived as a set of interconnected typefaces sharing the same proportions and underlying structure to create a coherent and balanced type family. The Slab was added alongside Sans Stencil and Slab Stencil cuts to extend the family's range of applications.

name: Independent Slab Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil variant of a slab serif designed for newspaper use; the stencil cuts and slab serifs together push it firmly into the display category despite sharing the underlying structure of the text-oriented Independent family.
summary: A stencil slab serif drawn as part of the broader Independent newspaper type system, sharing proportions with the rest of the family across five weights.
description: Part of the Independent family commissioned in 2013 for The Independent newspaper's print and digital editions, the Slab Stencil variant applies stencil cuts to the slab serif foundation shared across the wider suite. Five weights run from Light to Black, offering range for display and campaign applications. The parent family earned a D&AD Yellow Pencil in 2014 and the Typeface Design prize at the Tokyo TDC in 2015.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe the Independent family as a comprehensive suite of interconnected typefaces designed in 2013 for The Independent newspaper, sharing proportions and underlying structure across serif, sans and condensed masters. The Slab Stencil was added later to extend the family's range of applications, alongside Sans Stencil and Slab variants.

name: Independent Text
classification_notes: Designed specifically for newspaper body text in both print and digital, with proportions and structure calibrated for demanding reading conditions at small sizes. The Text cut sits within a broader family that includes Headline, Sans, Slab and Condensed variants.
summary: A text-optimised serif cut from a bespoke typeface family commissioned for The Independent newspaper in 2013, awarded a D&AD Yellow Pencil and the Tokyo TDC Typeface Design prize.
description: Drawn for the daily print and digital editions of The Independent, the Text cut shares proportions and underlying structure with its siblings across the wider Independent family, which spans headline, sans, condensed, slab and stencil variants. Five weights run from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, giving editors a full range for body text, standfirsts and pull quotes. The family received a D&AD Yellow Pencil in 2014 and the Typeface Design prize at the Tokyo Type Directors Club awards in 2015.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe the Independent family as a suite of interconnected typefaces designed in 2013 for The Independent newspaper's print and digital editions, sharing the same proportions and underlying structure to create a coherent and balanced type system. The Text cut is one of three master sets alongside Serif Headline and Sans, with Slab and Stencil variants added subsequently to extend the family's range of applications.

name: Independent Text Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil variant of a text serif, with interrupted strokes defining it as display-oriented despite its text-weight origins within a newspaper type system.
summary: A stencil serif subfamily within A2-Type's award-winning Independent family, originally commissioned for The Independent newspaper in 2013.
description: Part of a large interconnected type system designed for The Independent's print and digital editions, the Text Stencil subfamily applies stencil cuts across five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic. The wider family received a D&AD Yellow Pencil in 2014 and the Typeface Design prize at the Tokyo TDC in 2015. The stencil variants were added later to extend the family's range of applications beyond core editorial use.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Independent Text Stencil as part of a comprehensive suite of interconnected typefaces originally designed in 2013 for The Independent newspaper's daily print and digital editions, with all subfamilies sharing the same proportions and underlying structure to ensure coherence across the full system.

name: India
classification_notes: An elaborate display face inspired by traditional Indian Kolam floor drawing and pattern art, with decorative geometric patterning built into each letterform, placing it firmly in the ornamental display category.
summary: An ornamental display face commissioned by Wallpaper* magazine in 2011, drawing on the geometric pattern language of Indian Kolam floor art.
description: Originally created as a custom typeface for Wallpaper* magazine's 'Reborn in India' issue, the family takes its visual cues from Kolam, a traditional South Indian practice of drawing intricate patterns on floors using rice or chalk. Two cuts are offered: Regular and Regular Solid, the latter filling in the patterned voids of the letterforms. The uppercase-only character set limits its use to display headings and titling work where the decorative geometry can read at scale.
foundry_blurb: India was commissioned by Wallpaper* magazine as a custom font for their 'Reborn in India' issue in 2011. It is an elaborate display face inspired by traditional Indian floor drawing and pattern art known as Kolam, available in Regular and Regular Solid cuts.

name: Italian Sans
classification_notes: A wide, industrial grotesque drawn from 20th-century vernacular lettering specimens, placing it firmly in the industrial grotesque tradition rather than more refined humanist or geometric strands.
summary: A wide industrial grotesque drawn from 20th-century vernacular lettering specimens held at Central Saint Martins' Central Lettering Archive.
description: Sourced from historical specimens at the Central Lettering Archive at Central Saint Martins in London, the family carries the broad proportions and blunt character of 20th-century industrial signage lettering. Five weights run from Light to Bold, and a condensed subfamily extends its range to tighter settings. There are no italics, keeping it close to its vernacular origins.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Italian Sans as a wide, industrial style font inspired by historical specimens from the Central Lettering Archive at Central Saint Martins, London, calling it a prime example of a 20th-century vernacular grotesque, available in five weights from Light to Bold.

name: Italian Sans Condensed
classification_notes: An industrial-style condensed sans-serif drawn from historical lettering specimens, with a distinctive pseudo-monospaced rhythm achieved through proportional spacing.
summary: A condensed industrial sans drawn from archival lettering specimens held at the Central Lettering Archive at Central Saint Martins, with proportional spacing that mimics a monospaced rhythm.
description: Designed as a companion to the wider Italian Sans, this condensed cut takes its cues from historical specimens in the Central Lettering Archive at Central Saint Martins, London. Characters are proportionally spaced but carry a monospaced visual cadence, giving display and editorial settings an unusual mechanical regularity. Five weights run from Light to Bold with no italics, suited to tight-column work, posters and branding that calls for an industrial edge.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Italian Sans Condensed as a condensed companion to Italian Sans, noting its monospaced appearance achieved through proportional spacing. The family draws inspiration from historical specimens held at the Central Lettering Archive at Central Saint Martins, London, characterising it as an industrial-style typeface.

name: Kunstuff
classification_notes: A sturdy serif with pronounced serifs and generous counters, designed for editorial use in a magazine context. The combination of sturdy construction and open counters places it in a contemporary text serif category.
summary: A sturdy serif originally drawn in 2004 for the redesign of Kunstuff, a Danish art and crafts magazine, with open counters and pronounced serifs throughout.
description: Designed in 2004 as a bespoke typeface for a Danish art and crafts magazine, the family covers six weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic. Generously proportioned counters and pronounced serifs are its defining structural features, making it well suited to print editorial work. The range of weights gives it flexibility across text sizes and display settings.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Kunstuff as a sturdy serif font created in 2004 for the redesign of the Danish art and crafts magazine of the same name, noting its generously proportioned counters and pronounced serifs as its key features.

name: Kursiv
classification_notes: Kursiv is a casual handwritten script, described by the foundry as a simple, pared-back handwriting font with a minimal design aesthetic.
summary: A stripped-back handwritten script from A2-Type, offering a single weight with a basic glyph set and deliberately restrained design.
description: Kursiv offers a single Regular weight with no stylistic variation, designed to read as a plain, unaffected handwriting style. The foundry explicitly notes its minimalism and small file size, positioning it as a utilitarian choice rather than an expressive one. Those seeking more character are directed to A2-Type's Signature typeface instead.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Kursiv as a simple handwritten font with a consciously pared-back design, supported by a basic glyph set and small file size. They note that those looking for a livelier handwriting font should consider their Signature typeface instead.

name: LargePoint
classification_notes: A high-contrast, characterful grotesque sans designed primarily for headline and display use, available in both Sharp and Rounded (Soft) variants across a wide weight range.
summary: A headline-oriented grotesque developed with Matt Willey, offered in Sharp and Rounded styles across eight weights.
description: Originally conceived as a dynamic headline font in collaboration with designer Matt Willey, LargePoint has since expanded into two subfamilies: the sharp-edged LargePoint and the softened LargePoint Soft. Eight weights from Thin to Heavy make it flexible across editorial contexts, from fine display setting to heavy-impact titling. Its characterful construction positions it squarely at magazine and editorial designers working at large point sizes.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe LargePoint as a high-octane typeface that is bold, characterful and specifically tailored for editorial designers, originally created as a dynamic headline font in collaboration with Matt Willey, and now evolved into a suite of fonts offered in Sharp and Rounded styles.

name: LargePoint Soft
classification_notes: A rounded, high-contrast display sans with geometric underpinning, the Soft variant distinguishing itself from the Sharp sibling through softened terminals across an eight-weight range pitched squarely at headline use.
summary: A rounded headline sans developed by A2-Type in collaboration with Matt Willey, offering eight weights from Thin to Black for editorial display work.
description: The Soft variant of LargePoint trades the Sharp sibling's hard terminals for rounded ones, giving the family a distinct warmth without losing its headline energy. Eight weights run from Thin to Black, making it useful across cover lines, pull quotes and campaign headlines where visual hierarchy matters. Originally conceived as a dynamic display face, the suite format acknowledges that editorial designers need range, not just a single showstopper weight.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe LargePoint as originally created as a dynamic headline font in collaboration with Matt Willey, since evolved into a suite offering both Sharp and Rounded (Soft) styles. They characterise it as high-octane, bold and characterful, specifically tailored for editorial designers.

name: London
classification_notes: Described as a clean and mechanical serif inspired by Edward Johnston's Underground alphabet, placing it in the mechanical serif tradition with constructed, geometric-leaning letterforms rather than humanist or calligraphic ones.
summary: A mechanical serif in six weights with italics, drawn from the structural logic of Edward Johnston's London Underground alphabet.
description: Taking Edward Johnston's 1916 Underground lettering as a reference point, this serif trades calligraphic warmth for a clean, constructed character that sits comfortably in contemporary editorial and identity work. The family runs from Thin to Black with matching italics across all weights, giving it practical range without straying far from its disciplined core. The Johnston connection gives it a legible cultural anchor without leaning on nostalgia.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe London as a clean and mechanical serif font inspired in part by Edward Johnston's Underground alphabet, calling it a contemporary design that evokes the past but also embraces the future, available in six weights with corresponding italics.

name: Melissa Script
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an old-style handwriting typeface, placing it firmly in the script category as a casual or informal handwriting style rather than a formal calligraphic script.
summary: A single-weight old-style handwriting script suited to notes, messages and personal correspondence.
description: With one weight and a straightforward character set covering upper and lowercase Latin alongside figures and basic punctuation, Melissa Script keeps things deliberately simple. Its old-style handwriting character makes it a natural fit for greeting cards, personal notes and any context where warmth and informality are wanted. The narrow licensing scope of the page suggests it is positioned as a boutique retail offering rather than a workhorse text family.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Melissa Script as an old-style handwriting typeface ideal for messages, notes and correspondence, available in a single Regular weight.

name: Merk Dot
classification_notes: Derived from a linen-stitching instruction alphabet, the typeface uses a dot-based construction that places it firmly in the display category as a decorative, grid-structured face with craft origins.
summary: A dot-matrix display face drawn from a 19th-century Dollfus-Mieg & Cie linen-stitching instruction alphabet, extended by A2-Type into a full character set across three weights.
description: The source material, a 'Merk Stich' specimen featuring uppercase letters only, has been expanded by A2-Type to include lowercase, numerals and punctuation. Three weights, Light, Medium and Bold, give the family modest range for display use. A companion pixel sans, Merk PiX, is available as a single weight for those wanting a related but more screen-oriented option.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Merk Dot as inspired by the Dollfus-Mieg & Cie 'Merk Stich' instruction alphabet for linen stitching. The original specimen contained uppercase letters only, so A2-Type used their imagination to create the lowercase, numbers and additional characters, engineering this handmade source into a contemporary typeface across multiple styles.

name: Merk Pix
classification_notes: Derived from a historical embroidery instruction alphabet, the letterforms are built on a grid of discrete marks mimicking cross-stitch or pixel patterns, placing it firmly in the display/bitmap category.
summary: A pixel-style typeface derived from a Dollfus-Mieg & Cie cross-stitch instruction alphabet, extended to a full character set by A2-Type.
description: The original Merk Stich specimen offered only uppercase letters intended as a guide for linen stitching, so A2-Type invented the lowercase, numerals and punctuation to complete the family. The letterforms retain the rigid, grid-based quality of their embroidery origins while functioning as a contemporary display typeface. A companion family, Merk Dot, offers the same aesthetic in three weights using dot-pattern construction.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Merk Pix as inspired by the Dollfus-Mieg & Cie 'Merk Stich' linen-stitching instruction alphabet, noting that the original specimen contained only uppercase letters and that the studio used their imagination to build out the full character set including lowercase, numbers and sorts, engineering this handmade source into a contemporary multi-style typeface.

name: Microscopic
classification_notes: Derived from Gothic No. 3, an early 20th-century American gothic typeface, placing it squarely in the grotesque tradition. The mixed square and soft shapes with mechanically constructed letterforms are characteristic of late 19th and early 20th century American gothics.
summary: A duplexed grotesque revival of Gothic No. 3 from an 1908 Barnhart Brothers & Spindler specimen, built for legibility at small sizes on screen.
description: Based on Gothic No. 3 from an American type specimen of c. 1908, the design mixes square and soft forms with a mechanically constructed lowercase featuring distinctively pinched bowls on b, d, g, p and q. The family is duplexed, meaning all eight weights occupy identical widths, making it practical for UI work where text reflow must be avoided. An X Condensed subfamily extends the range for tighter settings.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Microscopic as a font inspired by Gothic No. 3 from the Barnhart Brothers & Spindler specimen of c. 1908, whose clarity at small point sizes suggested its name. They highlight the source typeface's amalgamation of square and soft shapes, the mechanically constructed lowercase with pinched bowls, and a duplexed system in which all weights occupy the same width, suited to screen and micro use cases.

name: Microscopic X Condensed
classification_notes: Drawn from Gothic No. 3, a late 19th/early 20th-century American gothic typeface, placing it firmly in the condensed grotesque tradition with mechanically constructed forms and squared-off details.
summary: A condensed grotesque revival of Gothic No. 3 from the 1908 Barnhart Brothers & Spindler specimen, built as a duplexed system for screen and small-size use.
description: Derived from an early 20th-century American gothic type specimen, the design preserves the source's unusual mix of square and soft construction, including the distinctively pinched bowls on letters like b, d and p. The entire family is duplexed, meaning every weight occupies identical advance widths, making it practical for interfaces where weight switching cannot disturb layout. Sixteen styles run across eight weights from Light to Heavy, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Microscopic X Condensed as inspired by Gothic No. 3 from the Barnhart Brothers & Spindler specimen of c. 1908, noting that its clarity at small sizes suggested the Microscopic name. They highlight the almost mechanically constructed lowercase with its original pinched bowls, and point to the duplexed design system as making it ideal for screen and micro use cases.

name: Monday
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a vernacular English serif typeface drawn from letterforms found on historical buildings in the City of London, placing it within a tradition of lapidary and inscriptional serif design.
summary: A vernacular English serif drawn from letterforms found on historical City of London buildings, originally commissioned for a 2003 Arnolfini Gallery exhibition.
description: Rooted in the inscriptional lettering of London's built environment, Monday carries the quiet authority of stone-cut or cast letterforms translated into a digital typeface. Six weights from Light to Black with matching italics give it a workable range for editorial and identity applications. Its origins in a gallery commission lend it a cultural specificity that distinguishes it from more generic historical revivals.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Monday as an example of a vernacular English serif typeface commonly found on historical buildings in the City of London. Originally created in 2003 for the Arnolfini Gallery's exhibition 'Apparition: the Action of Appearing', the family is described as elegant and is available across six weights with italics.

name: MVSEVM
classification_notes: A sans-serif whose uppercase draws directly from Roman imperial carved letterforms, giving it an inscriptional character that sits outside conventional grotesque or humanist classification. The lowercase is a contemporary companion designed to harmonise with the historically derived caps.
summary: A five-weight sans inspired by hand-carved Roman imperial capitals, pairing a historically grounded uppercase with a contemporary lowercase designed to match.
description: The uppercase draws on carved lettering from the Roman empire, giving the family a stately, monumental quality distinct from conventional grotesque or humanist models. A lowercase has been designed to sit alongside the historically informed caps without feeling anachronistic. Five weights from Regular to Black make it usable across both headline and text settings.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe MVSEVM as a sans alphabet in five weights designed for headlines and text, inspired by hand-carved capital letters from the Roman empire (27 BC to AD 395). They note that the lowercase has been carefully designed to match the historically inspired uppercase, with what they call a touch of contemporary cool.

name: New Airport Dot
designer: Margaret Calvert / Kinneir, Calvert, Tuhill Ltd.
classification_notes: A revival of illuminated dot-matrix lettering designed for British Airports Authority shop fascia signs circa 1970, placing it firmly in the display/signage category with a distinctive dot-grid construction.
summary: A revival of Margaret Calvert's circa-1970 dot-matrix lettering for British Airports Authority illuminated shop fascia signs, expanded from a single-weight lowercase to a full five-weight family.
description: Originally created by Kinneir, Calvert, Tuhill Ltd. for internally illuminated signage at Gatwick Airport, this revival restores and extends the original lettering with capitals, lining and non-lining numerals, and punctuation across five weights numbered 1 through 5. It sits apart from the better-known Calvert direction signage, being purpose-built for retail fascias rather than wayfinding. The result is a typeface with a specific institutional history that lends itself to display work, branding and anything drawing on mid-century British public design.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe New Airport Dot as a revival of lettering originally designed by Margaret Calvert and Kinneir, Calvert, Tuhill Ltd. around 1970 for the British Airports Authority, specifically for internally illuminated shop fascia signs rather than directional signage. Previously available only as lowercase in a single weight, the revival now includes capitals, numerals in lining and non-lining forms, and punctuation across five weights.

name: New Grotesk Round
classification_notes: A rounded geometric grotesque sharing proportions and systematic weight grading with its companion New Grotesk Square, distinguishing itself through fully rounded terminals rather than square ones.
summary: A rounded counterpart to New Grotesk Square, sharing the same proportions and seven-weight grading system with softened terminals throughout.
description: Built on the same structural model as New Grotesk Square, it matches its companion in proportions, systematic weight grading and optical contrast across seven weights numbered One through Seven. The key distinction is the rounded terminals, which give the family a softer register without departing from the neutral geometric framework. Well suited to branding or digital product work where a less austere tone is needed.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe New Grotesk Round as the perfect rounded counterpart to New Grotesk Square, built on the same model with shared proportions, systematic weight grading and optical contrast.

name: New Grotesk Square
classification_notes: A suite of condensed grotesque display fonts drawn from wood type references, organised by width rather than weight. Its display-oriented proportions and wood type heritage place it firmly in the display category.
summary: Seven condensed grotesque cuts drawn from historical wood type specimens, organised by increasing width from ONE to SEVEN.
description: Drawn from A2-Type's archive of letterpress wood type and printed ephemera, the family sequences seven fonts by width rather than weight, each sharing unified proportions across the range. The square-cornered forms give it a crisp, mechanical character suited to posters, headlines and identity work. A companion family, New Grotesk Round, offers the same system with softened terminals.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe New Grotesk Square as a suite of seven condensed fonts inspired by display wood type from their archive of historical specimens, printed ephemera and letterpress wood type collection. The family is designed with unified proportions across all widths, from ONE to SEVEN, to offer a varied palette for the modern designer, and is accompanied by a soft companion version called New Grotesk Round.

name: New Rail Alphabet
classification_notes: A revival of Margaret Calvert's original Rail Alphabet, itself a humanist sans designed for wayfinding legibility in British public infrastructure. The open apertures, moderate stroke contrast and letterform warmth place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A revival of Margaret Calvert's original Rail Alphabet, the humanist sans designed for British Rail and NHS hospital signage in the early 1960s.
description: Originally drawn by Calvert of Kinneir Calvert Associates for British public infrastructure, the typeface appeared first in National Health Service hospitals before rolling out across British Rail, Danish Rail and BAA airports. A2-Type's revival extends the family to six weights from Off White to Black, each with a matching italic, non-aligning numerals and a subset of Eastern European characters. A stencil variant is also available.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe New Rail Alphabet as a revival of the British Rail alphabet designed by Margaret Calvert in the early 1960s, originally produced in two versions as part of an integrated signing system. It appeared in NHS hospitals, British Rail and Danish Rail stations, and BAA airports. The revival adds six weights with italics, non-aligning numerals and Eastern European character support.

name: New Rail Alphabet Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil variant of a humanist sans-serif wayfinding alphabet, the stencil treatment places it firmly in the display category despite its functional origins in British Rail and NHS signage.
summary: A stencil adaptation of A2-Type's revival of the British Rail alphabet, originally designed by Margaret Calvert for UK public infrastructure in the early 1960s.
description: Derived from New Rail Alphabet, A2-Type's revival of the wayfinding typeface designed by Margaret Calvert of Kinneir Calvert Associates, this stencil version retains the clarity and authority of the original system used across NHS hospitals, British Rail, Danish Rail and BAA airports. Six weights run from Off White to Black, with non-aligning numerals and a subset of Eastern European characters. The stencil treatment gives the family a functional, utilitarian edge well suited to environmental graphics, editorial and identity work referencing British public design.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe New Rail Alphabet Stencil as based on their revival of the British Rail alphabet designed by Margaret Calvert of Kinneir Calvert Associates in the early 1960s. Originally drawn in two versions as part of an integrated signing system, it appeared first in UK National Health hospitals, then British Rail and Danish Rail stations and BAA airports. The stencil family is now available in six weights — Off White, White, Light, Medium, Bold and Black — with non-aligning numerals and a subset of Eastern European characters.

name: New Transport
classification_notes: Derived from the Kinneir and Calvert Transport lettering designed for British motorway signage in the 1960s, it exhibits the open, legibility-first characteristics of humanist sans-serifs engineered for wayfinding at speed.
summary: A digital adaptation of Kinneir and Calvert's 1960s motorway lettering, expanded into a full seven-weight family and adopted as the official typeface of UK government websites.
description: Originally drawn for British motorway and all-purpose road signs by Jock Kinneir and Margaret Calvert, the source material was digitised and extended by A2-Type into a fourteen-style family spanning Thin to Black with matching italics. A bespoke version was commissioned by the UK Government Digital Service in 2012 and remains the mandated typeface for all gov.uk websites. Its open apertures and generous x-height stem directly from the legibility demands of high-speed signage, which makes it equally capable in screen and document settings.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe New Transport as a digital adaptation of the Transport lettering created by Jock Kinneir and Margaret Calvert in the 1960s for the UK's new motorway network, later extended to all-purpose roads. A custom version was produced for the Government Digital Service in 2012 and is now the official typeface of all UK government websites, with the original signage letterforms still in use today with only minor modifications.

name: No Parking
classification_notes: A single-weight display typeface derived from hand-painted street lettering, placing it firmly in the vernacular display category with no serif or sans-serif classification applicable.
summary: A single-weight display face drawn from a hand-painted 'No Parking' sign spotted on a New York City sidewalk, winning the Tokyo TDC Prize in 2025.
description: Derived from a chance encounter with a local resident painting their own street sign, the typeface captures the idiosyncratic character of amateur hand-lettering, particularly an unconventional interpretation of the letter G. Only one weight exists, Bold, reflecting the source material's singular, assertive stroke. The font won the Tokyo Type Directors Club TDC Prize in 2025.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe No Parking as a vernacular alphabet inspired by spotting a New York City resident painting their own no-parking sign on the sidewalk. The unorthodox hand-painted letters, particularly the original G, posed a challenge in building a full font from a single line of public lettering. The typeface received the Tokyo Type Directors Club 2025 TDC Prize.

name: Nuacht Sans Condensed + X Condensed
classification_notes: Described by A2-Type as a humanist sans with an industrial character, the condensed and extra-condensed cuts were designed specifically for space-constrained newspaper contexts such as sports sections and information graphics.
summary: The condensed and extra-condensed members of the Nuacht family, designed for the Irish Independent's 2018 redesign and intended for sports pages and information graphics.
description: Part of a bespoke newspaper type system commissioned for the Irish Independent's 2018 redesign, these two width variants cover seven weights from Thin to Black and were built to handle the tight column measures of sports reporting and data-heavy graphics. The Condensed and X Condensed cuts share the humanist character of the broader Nuacht Sans while tolerating the compression needed for tabular and high-density editorial layouts. foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Nuacht (Irish for 'News') as a series of interconnected, versatile fonts created for the Irish Independent newspaper's 2018 redesign. The full system covers Serif Text and Headline for reading and display, Sans for captions, and Condensed and X Condensed variants for the Sports section and information graphics. name: Nuacht Sans
classification_notes: Described by A2-Type as a 'Humanistic' sans with an industrial character, designed to complement a calligraphic serif within a newspaper type system.
summary: A humanist sans designed as the caption and UI face within A2-Type's bespoke type system for the 2018 Irish Independent redesign.

foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Nuacht Sans as the sans component of a versatile newspaper font system designed for the Irish Independent's 2018 redesign. The system comprises serif fonts for long reading, display fonts for headlines, and this sans for captions, alongside condensed and extra-condensed variants for the sports section and information graphics. summary: A headline serif commissioned for the 2018 redesign of the Irish Independent, drawing on calligraphic lettering and SB Times Roman metal type references.
description: Part of a larger type system built for the Irish Independent, this headline cut sits alongside text, sans and condensed variants to cover every typographic role in a broadsheet environment. Eight weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, give editorial teams the range needed across front pages, section headers and feature spreads. The system includes Serif for long reading, Display for headlines, Sans for captions, and Condensed and X Condensed variants for sports and infographics. name: Nuacht Serif Text
classification_notes: Drawn from calligraphic ink lettering and early metal type samples of SB Times Roman, placing it in the transitional serif tradition optimised for continuous reading at text sizes.

foundry_blurb: Nuacht, the Irish word for news, is a connected type system designed for the Irish Independent's 2018 redesign. name: Ny. Sans
classification_notes: A sans-serif companion to the Ny. family, designed as part of a custom type system for The New York Times Magazine redesign; its sharpness and contrast draw influence from the Gothic Blackletter logotype of the magazine, giving it an editorial grotesque character.
summary: A ten-weight grotesque sans designed by Henrik Kubel as part of the custom type system created for the 2015 redesign of The New York Times Magazine.

The name references both New York and the Danish word for 'new', reflecting the background of principal designer Henrik Kubel.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Ny. as a family of custom typefaces originally designed for the 2015 redesign of The New York Times Magazine. The Gothic Blackletter logotype of the magazine influenced the sharpness and contrast of the designs, with the name 'Ny' referencing both New York and the Danish word for 'new', the birthplace of principal designer Henrik Kubel.

name: Ny. Serif Condensed Stencil
classification_notes: A condensed stencil cut of a serif family inspired by Mazarin and the New York Times Magazine logotype, placing it firmly in the display category with high contrast and sharp detailing drawn from blackletter influences.

The stencil treatment and tight condensed stance make it purpose-built for headline and display use at large sizes. It sits within a broad editorial system that spans text serifs, slab variants, and sans companions.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe the serif inspiration for the Ny. family as rooted in Stephenson Blake's Mazarin, a Garamond-like metal typeface also known as Astree from Deberny & Peignot. They note that the Gothic Blackletter design of the New York Times Magazine logotype was equally influential, particularly in the sharpness and contrast of the Display and Headline fonts. name: Ny. Serif Headline
classification_notes: Rooted in Stephenson Blake's Mazarin and Garamond-like references, with sharpness and contrast drawn from Gothic Blackletter traditions; optimised explicitly for headline use at display sizes.

The name 'Ny' nods to New York as the typeface's first home, and to 'New' in Danish, Kubel's mother tongue.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Ny. Serif Headline as part of a family of custom typefaces originally designed for the 2015 redesign of The New York Times Magazine. name: Ny. Slab
classification_notes: Ny. Slab is an upright slab serif designed as part of a wider type system rooted in the Garamond-like Mazarin metal typeface, with sharpness and contrast drawn from Gothic Blackletter tradition, placing it firmly in the slab category with editorial refinement.
summary: A nine-weight slab serif drawn by Henrik Kubel as part of the custom type system created for the 2015 redesign of The New York Times Magazine.

The serif inspiration comes from Stephenson Blake's Mazarin, while the sharpness and contrast draw on the Gothic Blackletter logotype of the magazine itself. The name 'Ny' references New York and also means 'New' in Danish, the birthplace of principal designer Henrik Kubel.

name: Ny. Slab Sans
classification_notes: Ny. Slab Sans removes the serifs from the Ny. Slab Serif while retaining its proportions and weight range, placing it as a sans-serif companion within a slab-rooted family system originally designed for editorial use.

Nine weights from Thin to Black allow a consistent typographic texture alongside the serifed variants in the same system. The tighter setting makes it a practical choice for editorial contexts where the Slab Serif's feel is wanted without the additional stroke detail.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe the Ny. Slab Sans as built on the proportions and weight range of the Ny. Slab Serif, offering a tighter setting with a familiar look. The Ny. family was originally created for the 2015 redesign of The New York Times Magazine, with the serif drawing inspiration from Stephenson Blake's Mazarin and the Gothic Blackletter of the Magazine's logotype. The name Ny. references New York and the Danish word for 'New', a nod to principal designer Henrik Kubel's birthplace.

name: Ny. Slab Skyscraper
classification_notes: An extreme ultra-condensed slab serif designed for display use at large sizes, part of a family of editorial serifs created for The New York Times Magazine. Its highly exaggerated proportions place it firmly in the display slab subcategory.
summary: An ultra-condensed display slab serif commissioned by The New York Times Magazine, taken further than its sister cut Ny. Slab Tower for maximum vertical exaggeration.

It takes the proportions of Ny. Slab Tower and pushes them further still, producing a tall, tightly compressed slab suited to large-scale headlines and poster settings. Available in a single Black weight, it is a specialist cut built for moments where vertical drama is the editorial intent.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Ny. Slab Tower and Ny. Slab Skyscraper as two special edition slab serifs originally designed for The New York Times Magazine. Tower is a taller, more condensed version of Ny. Slab, and Skyscraper takes that exaggeration even further. Both were commissioned by Gail Bichler and Matt Willey.

name: Ny. Slab Tower
classification_notes: A tall, condensed slab serif with an exaggerated vertical proportion, designed specifically for editorial display use in The New York Times Magazine. Ny. Slab Skyscraper pushes the condensation further still.

Slab family into increasingly tall, narrow display territory. Tower is a condensed upright of Ny. Slab; Skyscraper takes that compression to a more extreme ratio. Both are available in Black only, suited to large-scale editorial headlines where vertical rhythm and column-fitting matter.
foundry_blurb: Ny. Slab Tower and Ny. Slab Skyscraper are described as two special edition slab serifs originally designed for The New York Times Magazine, commissioned by Gail Bichler and Matt Willey. name: Ny. Slab Wide

Available in a single Black weight, it is built for large-scale display use where boldness and width are paramount. The name references New York, where the family debuted, and the Danish word for 'new', a nod to Kubel's origins.

name: Ny. Text

Text as a serif whose inspiration lies in Stephenson Blake's Mazarin and Deberny & Peignot's Astree, with additional influence from the Gothic Blackletter of The New York Times Magazine logotype, particularly in its sharpness and contrast. name: Ominiqua

Ten styles cover Thin through Bold with matching italics, giving it enough range for editorial and branding work. The historical source lends it a quiet formality without tipping into pastiche.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Ominiqua as inspired by a detail from a stone-carved memorial plate in the south of France, calling it a hardworking, versatile serif that combines contemporary design with classical details.

name: Popular
classification_notes: Derived from Barnhart Bros. & Spindler's 'Lining Gothic Number 111' (1907), Popular belongs to the American gothic grotesque tradition, characterised by subtle stroke contrast and a naturally condensed construction.
summary: A revival of Barnhart Bros. & Spindler's 1907 'Lining Gothic Number 111', expanded into a six-weight family with italics for contemporary headline and text use.
description: Drawn from a single-weight specimen in Barnhart Bros. & Spindler's No. 9 catalogue of 1907, the design retains the subtly contrasted strokes and naturally condensed proportions of its source while adding five further weights plus italics throughout. The unusual details of the original cuts survive into the revival, giving it an oddly contemporary feel that works at display sizes and in running text. A solid choice for editorial work that needs a period American gothic without slavish historicism.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Popular as a modern sans serif inspired by a page from Barnhart Bros. & Spindler's 1907 specimen No. 9, where the face appeared as 'Lining Gothic Number 111' in a single weight with no italic. They note that the subtly contrasted letters and condensed proportions give it an oddly contemporary look suited to headlines and running text alike.

name: Prismax
classification_notes: A five-line inline display typeface designed exclusively for large sizes and headlines, following the tradition of optical and multi-line alphabets rooted in Koch's Prisma (1931). Its decorative striped construction places it firmly in the display category.
summary: A five-line humanist display face drawing on Rudolf Koch's Prisma (1931) and Akihiko Seki's Aki Lines (1970/72), built for headlines and large sizes.
description: Prismax continues a lineage of optical and multi-line alphabets that peaked in popularity during the 1970s, tracing its roots directly to Koch's Prisma. The graded parallel lines across each letterform give the face a layered, dimensional quality, and a set of interlocking ligatures adds further typographic character for letter pairs such as OC, HD and NNE. Available in a single Regular weight, it is a specialist tool for headline-scale display work rather than a general-purpose family.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Prismax as a contemporary five-line humanist font designed for headlines and large point sizes, inspired by Rudolf Koch's Prisma alphabet (1931) and Akihiko Seki's Aki Lines font (1970/72), the latter famously used in Microsoft's 1975 logotype. The graded lines are said to create extra dimension and a sense of dynamism, while multiple interlocking ligatures add typographic interest. The foundry situate it within a long tradition of optical and multi-line alphabets, many of which enjoyed widespread popularity during the 1970s.

name: Recognition

Eight weights run from Thin to Heavy, each paired with a slanted italic. The construction is solid and unpretentious, suited to institutional, editorial and wayfinding contexts.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Recognition as originally designed in 2003 as a stand-alone sans font in a single weight for the catalogue accompanying the 'Recognition' exhibition at the Arnolfini Gallery in Bristol. foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Reg.Modn as historical, modern, geometric and legible, drawing inspiration from their own Regular, Schwiss and London typefaces to create a stylish variant that feels grounded in typographic history yet distinctly contemporary. Variable fonts are available for both Roman and Italic styles, covering predefined weights from Regular to Black and everything in between, in GX/TTF format for designers working on digital platforms.

name: Regular
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a geometric sans serif inspired by hot metal geometric classics Memphis, Karnak, Stymie and Futura, placing it squarely in the geometric sans tradition with a modernist lineage.
summary: A geometric sans rooted in hot metal modernism, drawing on Memphis, Karnak, Stymie and Futura, with a sprawling family of subfamilies covering soft, slab, mono and inline variants.
description: Inspired by the canonical hot metal geometrics of the twentieth century, the family extends well beyond a standard type system into distinct subfamilies including Regular XL for small sizes and screen use, Regular Soft for a warmer feel, Regular Mono for a default-adjacent aesthetic, a slab variant and an inline cut. foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Regular as a geometric sans serif typeface inspired by the popular hot metal fonts Memphis, Karnak, Stymie and Futura, firmly rooted within the modernist tradition with a contemporary take. Regular XL features a large x-height suited to small point sizes and screen use, while Regular Soft offers a friendlier alternative and Regular Mono provides a more distinctly default look.

name: Regular Inline
classification_notes: Derived from Regular Slab, this is a display slab serif with inline stroke detailing, designed for headline use. The inline treatment and slab serifs place it firmly in the display subcategory.
summary: A single-weight inline display slab, drawn originally as a custom brand typeface for The Athletic sports news site in 2019.

Its application is narrow: editorial display, sports branding and signage where the inline detail can read clearly at scale.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Regular Inline as a stylish inline version of their popular Regular Slab, originally designed as a custom brand typeface for The Athletic sports news site, with art direction by Gretel Design and Strategy Studio in Brooklyn, New York, in 2019.

name: Regular Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced geometric sans inspired by hot metal faces including Futura, Memphis and Stymie, placing it firmly in the geometric tradition adapted for fixed-width use.
summary: A monospaced companion to A2-Type's Regular, drawing on Futura, Memphis and Stymie to bring geometric hot-metal warmth to a fixed-width format.

foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Regular Mono as a companion style to their Regular typeface, a geometric sans serif inspired by hot metal fonts including Memphis, Karnak, Stymie and Futura. Variable fonts are available on request, and the licensing structure covers print, web, app, broadcast and logo use.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe RGB as built for clarity, consistency and scale, performing the same across systems, platforms and products without surprise or deviation. Designed for data-driven environments and high-frequency use, it supports repeated touchpoints with quiet reliability, staying neutral and precise so that content and interface can lead. Made for products used by millions, it provides a stable typographic foundation that holds up under constant use.

name: RV Shaddick
classification_notes: A revival of gold leaf shop-sign lettering with Art Deco proportions, designed exclusively for display use across two width masters with conventional and backslanted italic companions.
summary: A revival of Art Deco gold leaf shop-sign lettering from East London, available in two widths with conventional and backslanted italics.
description: Drawn from an old East London shop sign, the design combines precisely rendered Art Deco lettering with a contemporary edge across two master widths, One and Two. Each width comes with a standard 20° italic and a backslanted -20° italic, the latter a historical curiosity found in early specimens such as Figgins Seven Lines Pica, No. 2. The family ships as six pre-defined static styles plus a variable font spanning both width axes.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe RV Shaddick as a revival of a beautiful example of gold leaf lettering from an old East London shop sign. The Art Deco design is fused with a contemporary edge and is offered in two master widths, each accompanied by a conventional italic and a backslanted italic, available as six static styles or a single variable font.

name: Schwiss
classification_notes: Explicitly modelled on Akzidenz-Grotesk and Helvetica, two canonical European grotesques; monolinear strokes, closed apertures, and large x-height place it firmly in the grotesque tradition.
summary: A 14-style grotesque that draws on Akzidenz-Grotesk and Helvetica, retaining closed apertures and a large x-height in a clean, monolinear package.
description: Seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, built around the visual grammar of the two dominant European modernist grotesques. Closed C, G and S forms, square punctuation and monolinear strokes give it a familiar utilitarian character without being a direct revival of either source. Aimed at designers who want the reassurance of the grotesque tradition with a slightly fresher set of curves.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Schwiss as a solid and versatile family inspired by Akzidenz-Grotesk and Helvetica, crafted for designers seeking a new utilitarian Grotesk. Details such as closed apertures, a large x-height, square punctuation and clean monolinear shapes have been retained and reimagined across 14 fonts that they say look both reassuringly familiar and refreshingly new.

name: Signature
classification_notes: Signature is a digitised handwritten script, described by the foundry as loose, quirky and handcrafted, placing it firmly in the casual or informal script category rather than formal calligraphic styles.
summary: A digitised casual handwriting face from A2-Type, characterised by loose, lively letterforms with a handcrafted feel.
description: Drawn from hand lettering rather than formal calligraphic tradition, this single-weight script sits at the informal end of the spectrum. The foundry itself notes its liveliness, directing users who want something more structured towards their Kursiv face. Available in a single Regular weight, it suits packaging, branding and editorial contexts where an authentic handmade quality is needed.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Signature as a digitised version of a hand drawn script face, characterised by a loose, quirky and handcrafted design. They suggest their Kursiv typeface as an alternative for those seeking a less lively and more constructed handwriting style.

name: SUBJECT
classification_notes: Described as a geometric sans with grotesque qualities, drawing on hand-carved stone lettering for its source material while presenting a contemporary, slightly humanised form.
summary: A geometric sans drawn from hand-carved stone lettering found in the French mountains, recast as a contemporary grotesque with subtle humanist detail.
description: Taking its cue from carved mountain stonework rather than the drawing board, SUBJECT sits somewhere between geometric precision and the warmth of a hand-cut letter. Six weights run from Light to Black with no italics, giving it a restrained palette suited to identity and editorial use. The unusual source material lends it details that distinguish it from standard geometric grotesques without making it eccentric.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe SUBJECT as a geometric sans inspired by hand-carved stones found in the French mountains, restored and reimagined as a contemporary grotesque. They note subtle details and typographic finesse that set it apart from familiar alphabets, calling it a unique sans serif with a human touch.

name: Subway 56
classification_notes: A single-style sans drawn from mid-century New York transit signage, placing it firmly in the vernacular or sign-painter tradition of sans-serif lettering rather than any formal grotesque or geometric lineage.
summary: Drawn from mid-century New York Transit Museum signage and originally commissioned by art director Matt Willey for the New York Times Magazine in 2015, then expanded in 2022 into four height variants.
description: Originally a single-style commission for the New York Times Magazine 'Walking' issue, the typeface takes its letterforms directly from signs held in the Brooklyn-based New York Transit Museum collection. In 2022 A2-Type expanded it into a multi-height family of four masters — X, XX, XXX and XXXX — giving designers control over vertical proportion while retaining the original's period character. The result works across editorial, signage and display applications at both large and small sizes.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Subway 56 as a single-style sans with plenty of character, originally commissioned in 2015 by art director Matt Willey for the New York Times Magazine 'Walking' issue, with its letterforms influenced by signs from the collection of the New York Transit Museum. In 2022 they expanded it into a multi-height typeface with four master styles — X, XX, XXX and XXXX — to broaden its typographic range.

name: Sunday Clarendon
classification_notes: Directly descended from the Egyptian/Clarendon slab serif tradition, with bracket-free angular serifs and blunt terminals characteristic of early nineteenth-century Egyptian types.
summary: A Clarendon-lineage slab serif with angular serifs and blunt terminals, spanning seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics.
description: Drawn in the tradition of early Egyptian typefaces, Sunday Clarendon uses angular serifs and blunt terminals to give the family a mechanical, forthright character. The weight range is broad enough to cover both text and display work: the middle weights are tuned for body copy while the extreme ends, Thin and Black, are pitched squarely at headline use. Fourteen styles in total, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Sunday Clarendon as bold, mechanistic and characterful, designed in the vein of early Egyptian typefaces including its namesake Clarendon. The foundry highlight its angular serifs and blunt terminals, and note that the extreme weights are display-orientated while the middle weights are tailored for text use.

name: Typewriter
classification_notes: A digitised typewriter face derived directly from an Olivetti Lettera 22 specimen, with the fixed-pitch, monospaced character common to typewriter-style fonts.
summary: A digital typewriter face drawn from a single type proof produced by a discarded Olivetti Lettera 22, expanded into three weights with matching italics.
description: The starting point was a found type proof from an Olivetti Lettera 22 left behind in the A2-Type studio, which the foundry used as reference to build a distinctive digital typewriter family. The result goes well beyond mere digitisation: the glyph set has been enlarged, the family extended to Regular, Medium and Bold, and complementary italic styles added throughout. The monospaced character and mechanical texture of the original machine are preserved, making it well suited to editorial work that calls for an authentic typewritten register.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Typewriter as inspired by a found Olivetti Lettera 22 typewriter abandoned by a former studio tenant. A single type proof from the machine served as the starting point, which they then developed into their own digital typewriter font with an expanded glyph set, additional weights and complementary italic styles.

name: Typewriter Contrast
classification_notes: Derives from A2-Type's Typewriter family and retains the monospaced, fixed-pitch character of typewriter-style fonts, distinguished by square terminals and stroke contrast uncommon in the genre.
summary: A contrasted, square-terminal reworking of A2-Type's Typewriter face, spanning three weights with italics for headline and text use.
description: Built on the foundation of A2 Typewriter, this variant introduces stroke contrast and square terminals that shift the familiar typewriter aesthetic towards something more considered and contemporary. Three weights from Regular to Bold each carry matching italics, giving the family enough range for headlines, pull-quotes and running text alike. The mid-century modern reference is present without tipping into pastiche.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Typewriter Contrast as a quirky iteration of their A2 Typewriter font, featuring square terminals and contrasted weight. They position it as a stylish rethink of the typewriter model that feels contemporary yet evokes the mid-century modern era, suited to headlines, pull-quotes, running text, folios and captions.

name: Varming
classification_notes: Geometric construction with chamfered corners gives it a distinctive technical character; the chamfering softens at small sizes, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with an engineered twist.
summary: A geometric sans with chamfered corners, originally drawn in 2013 as a custom face for engineering consultancy Steensen Varming.
description: Originally a commission for Steensen Varming, an international building services and engineering consultancy, this geometric sans is defined by its delicately chamfered corners. The detail reads as sharp and distinctive at display sizes, softening to a rounded quality at text scales. Five weights from Regular to Heavy cover the range for corporate identity use.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe Varming as a geometric design with delicately chamfered corners, originally created as a custom font family for Steensen Varming in 2013. They note that the chamfering is distinctive at large sizes yet appears soft and rounded at smaller sizes.

name: WEA Display
classification_notes: WEA Display is a high-contrast display serif designed for large-size editorial use in a newspaper context, optimised for headlines and prominent typesetting rather than continuous text.
summary: A display serif drawn for Weekendavisen, the Danish weekly broadsheet, as part of a type system that won the World's Best Designed Newspaper award twice.
description: Commissioned by Weekendavisen and rooted in the paper's lineage back to Berlingske Tidende (1749), WEA Display provides thirteen styles from Light to Black with matching italics for headline work. It forms part of a broader system alongside WEA Text, WEA Hairline, WEA Sans, WEA Sans Condensed and WEA Sans X Condensed, built for both print and screen. The system received the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021 and the redesign it underpins was named World's Best Designed Newspaper by the Society for News Design in both 2021 and 2026.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe WEA Display as part of a comprehensive family of interconnected fonts commissioned by Weekendavisen, a Danish weekly broadsheet with 250,000 readers. Rooted in the paper's lineage back to Berlingske Tidende (1749), the system spans Hairline, Display, Text and Sans, designed for both print and on-screen reading. The redesign was named World's Best Designed Newspaper by SND in 2021 and 2026, and the type system received the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021.

name: WEA
classification_notes: The family includes a hairline, display, and text serif alongside a sans companion, consistent with a transitional or classical newspaper serif lineage rooted in Berlingske Tidende (1749). The hairline and display cuts suggest high-contrast modulated strokes typical of transitional classification.
summary: A bespoke type system commissioned for Danish weekly broadsheet Weekendavisen, spanning hairline, display, text and sans subfamilies for print and screen.
description: Commissioned by Weekendavisen, a Danish weekly with 250,000 readers and a lineage traceable to Berlingske Tidende (1749), the system comprises four distinct subfamilies — Hairline, Display, Text and Sans — with the Sans extending into Condensed and X Condensed widths. Designed for both print and digital reading, the project won the SND World's Best Designed Newspaper award in 2021 and 2026, and the type system received the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021. A thorough editorial system built to carry the visual identity of a historic publication across all formats.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe WEA as a comprehensive family of interconnected fonts commissioned by Weekendavisen, the Danish weekly broadsheet with 250,000 readers. Rooted in the paper's lineage back to Berlingske Tidende (1749), the system spans Hairline, Display, Text and Sans, designed for both print and on-screen reading. The redesign was named World's Best Designed Newspaper by the SND in 2021 and 2026, and the type system received the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021.

name: WEA Sans
classification_notes: A newspaper sans drawn for editorial use across print and screen, with humanist proportions suited to continuous reading across a range of weights and widths.
summary: The sans-serif arm of a bespoke type system commissioned by Weekendavisen, a Danish weekly broadsheet with roots tracing to 1749, awarded World's Best Designed Newspaper in 2021 and 2026.
description: Designed as part of a four-subfamily system for Weekendavisen alongside WEA Text, WEA Display and WEA Hairline, the Sans runs across nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics and extends into Condensed and X Condensed cuts. The full system won the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021 and the newspaper's redesign has been recognised twice by the Society for News Design as the world's best designed paper. Suited to print and screen across UI, headline and body roles.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe WEA Sans as part of a comprehensive family of interconnected fonts commissioned by Weekendavisen, the Danish weekly broadsheet with 250,000 readers. Rooted in the paper's lineage back to Berlingske Tidende (1749), the system spans Hairline, Display, Text and Sans, designed for both print and on-screen reading. The redesign was named World's Best Designed Newspaper by the SND in 2021 and 2026, and the type system received the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021.

name: WEA Sans Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed sans-serif designed as part of a newspaper type system, sitting in the grotesque tradition with a practical, editorial sensibility suited to tight column widths in print and on screen.
summary: The condensed sans-serif component of a bespoke type system commissioned for Weekendavisen, the Danish weekly broadsheet, part of a redesign that won World's Best Designed Newspaper at SND in 2021.
description: Nine weights from Thin to Black make this a versatile condensed grotesque built for dense editorial layouts across print and screen. It sits within a larger type system encompassing Hairline, Display, Text and Sans variants, all rooted in the paper's lineage back to Berlingske Tidende (1749). The full system received the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021, and the newspaper redesign it anchors was named World's Best Designed Newspaper twice over.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe WEA Sans Condensed as part of a comprehensive family of interconnected fonts commissioned by Weekendavisen, the Danish weekly broadsheet with 250,000 readers. The system spans Hairline, Display, Text and Sans variants designed for both print and on-screen reading, rooted in the paper's lineage back to Berlingske Tidende (1749). The redesign was named World's Best Designed Newspaper by SND in 2021 and 2026, with the type system receiving the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021.

name: WEA Sans X Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed grotesque sans designed as part of a large newspaper type system, the extra-condensed width serving dense editorial layouts across print and screen.
summary: The extra-condensed arm of the WEA type system, commissioned for Weekendavisen and awarded World's Best Designed Newspaper at SND in 2021 and 2026.
description: Nine weights from Thin to Black in an extra-condensed grotesque, designed as part of a large interconnected type system for Weekendavisen, the Danish weekly broadsheet. The broader WEA family spans Hairline, Display, Text and Sans variants, built to serve both print and screen across a paper with roots in Berlingske Tidende dating to 1749. The system received the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021 alongside back-to-back SND World's Best Designed Newspaper recognition.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe WEA Sans X Condensed as part of a comprehensive family of interconnected fonts commissioned by Weekendavisen, a Danish weekly broadsheet with 250,000 readers. The system spans Hairline, Display, Text and Sans subfamilies designed for print and on-screen reading, with the redesign winning World's Best Designed Newspaper at SND in both 2021 and 2026, and the type system receiving the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021.

name: WEA Text
classification_notes: A newspaper text serif designed for sustained reading at small sizes, built as part of a larger editorial type system encompassing Display, Hairline and Sans cuts, consistent with a contemporary transitional or humanist text serif approach.
summary: The text serif component of a complete editorial type system commissioned by Danish weekly broadsheet Weekendavisen, winner of World's Best Designed Newspaper in 2021 and 2026.
description: Designed for Weekendavisen, a Danish broadsheet tracing its lineage to Berlingske Tidende (1749), WEA Text sits within a four-part system alongside Display, Hairline and Sans subfamilies. Fourteen styles span Light to Black, each with a matching italic, tuned for legibility at small print sizes and on-screen. The broader type system received the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021, and the newspaper redesign was named World's Best Designed Newspaper by SND twice.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe WEA Text as part of a comprehensive family of interconnected fonts commissioned by Weekendavisen, a Danish weekly broadsheet with 250,000 readers. Rooted in the paper's history back to Berlingske Tidende (1749), the system spans Hairline, Display, Text and Sans, designed for both print and on-screen reading. The redesign was named World's Best Designed Newspaper by SND in 2021 and 2026, with the type system also receiving the Tokyo TDC Award in 2021.

name: Zadie
classification_notes: Zadie is a decorative headline typeface with an inline/solid variant, inspired by Edwardian cast ironwork. Its ornamental character and headline-only application place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A decorative headline typeface drawn from Edwardian cast ironwork, with an inline Regular and a filled Solid cut.
description: Originally appearing in Vogue UK's Winter 2005 issue, the typeface later received a commissioned cut for Penguin Press's New York edition of Zadie Smith's 'On Beauty'. Available in two styles, Regular and Solid Regular, it is intended strictly for headline use where its ornamental ironwork character can read at large sizes. The family is narrow in scope but specific in purpose, suited to editorial mastheads and literary cover design.
foundry_blurb: Zadie is described by A2-Type as a decorative headline font inspired by Edwardian-era cast ironwork. An early version appeared in Vogue UK in 2005, and Penguin Press (New York) subsequently commissioned a cut for the cover of Zadie Smith's 'On Beauty'. The typeface is available in two styles: Regular and Solid Regular.

name: 8 Point Lining Typewriter No.1
classification_notes: A digital revival of a typewriter-style alphabet from a 1908 type specimen, with fixed-width letterforms and characteristic typewriter aesthetics including exaggerated details on key characters.
summary: A single-style digital revival of an 8 Point Lining Typewriter alphabet from a Barnhart Brothers & Spindler specimen of c. 1908, with distinctly characterful details throughout.
description: Drawn from a typewriter alphabet in the Barnhart Brothers & Spindler type specimen published around 1908, the design preserves idiosyncratic details that set it apart from generic typewriter revivals. Standout features include a flamboyant leg on the capital R, a pronounced curve on the lowercase t, an exaggerated overhanging f, and flourished numerals. A2-Type note it reads particularly well at small point sizes, making it suited to editorial contexts where period texture is needed without sacrificing legibility.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe this as a digital interpretation of the 8 Point Lining Typewriter alphabet from the Barnhart Brothers & Spindler typeface specimen, published in the US around 1908. They highlight unusual details including the flamboyant leg on the capital R, the pronounced curve of the lowercase t, the exaggerated overhang on the lowercase f, and flourished numerals, noting the typeface has a distinct and pleasing appearance especially at small point sizes.

name: 16-BIT
classification_notes: Designed explicitly as a bitmap font for low-resolution digital displays at 16 pixels and smaller, with no kerning or OpenType features, placing it firmly in the bitmap/pixel mono category.
summary: A single-weight bitmap font built for legibility at 16 pixels and below on black and white low-resolution screens.
description: Designed specifically for micro-size rendering on low-resolution digital displays, the font ships in one style with no kerning or OpenType features. The extended Latin and Cyrillic character set covers a broad range of languages within its deliberately constrained construction. Legibility at very small sizes is the sole design objective, and the stripped-back approach serves that purpose well.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe 16-BIT as a new bitmap font designed for use at micro-size (16 pixels and smaller) on black and white low-resolution digital displays. It comes in a single Regular style with an extended Latin and Cyrillic character set, and contains no kerning or OpenType features, keeping it basic in look, simple in construction, and highly legible.

classification_notes: Inspired directly by chiselled datestone lettering from a railway building in East London, the typeface sits firmly in the display category with its carved, inscriptional character and two styles — Solid and Chiseled — designed for impact at large sizes.
summary: A display typeface drawn from just four numerals carved into a Victorian railway datestone in East London, available in Solid and Chiseled styles.
description: Derived from the carved lettering of a datestone above a former East London railway entrance, this is not a historical revival but a contemporary interpretation of a very specific source. Available in one weight with two styles — Solid and Chiseled — the latter preserving the incised quality of the hand-carved original. Despite its display character and expressive quirks at large sizes, A2-Type designed it to remain legible at small point sizes.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe 1903 as inspired by carved lettering found on a datestone above the entrance to what was once Homerton Terrace Railway in East London. Working from only four numerals as source material, they frame the project as an open-ended exercise in translating a unique historical lettering style into a modern typeface — legible at small sizes, impactful and characterful at large ones. It is available in one weight and two styles: Solid and Chiseled, the latter paying homage to the hand-carved original.

name: 1903 Chiseled
classification_notes: Derived from hand-carved stone lettering on a Victorian datestone, the chiseled cuts and incised stroke modulation place this firmly in the display/incised category rather than any conventional serif or sans-serif tradition.
summary: A display typeface drawn from four numerals carved into a Victorian railway datestone in East London, reinterpreted into a full alphabet with a deliberate chiseled texture.
description: Born from the inscription above the former Homerton Terrace Railway station, 1903 Chiseled takes just four numeral forms as its source and extrapolates an entire contemporary typeface without attempting a historical revival. Available in one weight, it sits alongside the companion Solid style, with the Chiseled cut preserving the hand-carved quality of the original stonework. It reads cleanly at small sizes but carries enough idiosyncrasy to hold its own at display scale.
foundry_blurb: A2-Type describe 1903 as inspired by carved lettering on a datestone above the entrance to what was once Homerton Terrace Railway in East London. Working from only four numerals as source material, they frame the project as both daunting and liberating, aiming for a typeface that is neither a strict revival nor a generic interpretation, but an open-ended exploration of how a unique historical lettering style translates into a modern, readable typeface with distinctive character at large sizes.
